1.1: Lamuel, king . Most exegetes agree that this Lamuel , whose name in Hebrew means who belongs to God , Or who has God with him , Or consecrated God , or finally consecrated to God , is none other than Solomon, especially since there has never been a king of Israel or Judah who bore that name, and the work of a pagan prince would never have been inserted into the canon of sacred Scriptures.
1.2: object of my wishes ; That is to say, I have wished for it with so many ardent desires.
1.4: That is to say, a haughty, arrogant look. gorgeous heart ; arrogance, pride.
1.10: A strong woman . The Fathers considered this strong woman like the figure of the Virgin Mary and the Church of Jesus Christ. They explained the rest of the chapter in this sense.
1.9: , bears the inscription: "Words of King Lamuel." This short piece is written in verse with a synonymous and very regular parallelism.
1.8: its place , That is to say, his homeland, his dwelling place, a man is like a bird that abandons its nest, exposed to a thousand dangers and a thousand trials. The ancient Hebrews were very attached to their homeland and did not like to travel; they were held there, firstly, by the reason of their religion, the perfect practice of which was concentrated in Palestine; secondly, by the danger of idolatry, which was widespread throughout the world; and finally by the very nature of their soil, which was one of the best in the world.
1.19: The trace of man , etc.; that is to say, the way in which he reached manhood: how from weak, mute, stupid, ignorant, simple, he became strong, speaking, prudent, skillful, enterprising, attached to his pleasures and interests.
1.20: . It dwells in the walls of houses as well as in rocks, and "can be taken with the hand," as the text says.
1.22: , each beginning with one of these letters, placed according to the usual order. It is a praise of the strong woman, an ideal portrait as conceived by the wise man, inspired by the Holy Spirit. «Solomon does not find the strong woman on a throne, nor in a sumptuous palace, nor in the councils of kings, nor in the midst of human assemblies; rather, he seeks her in the common and ordinary condition in which God has placed woman, that is, in her role as wife and mother, mistress of the house, even woman of the fields, for it is only in this simple and modest role that woman is called to show herself strong, which means intelligent, active, careful, provident, orderly in all things, solely occupied with her duties, and accomplished in virtue.« The portrait that Solomon painted of this woman is
1.13: She did not buy the canvases and fabrics ready-made, but she worked them herself with her own hands.
1.24: Hates his soul ; That is to say, his life, since he risks losing it. According to Mosaic law, an accomplice of a thief was brought before the judge, who adjured him by the living God to declare the perpetrator of the theft; if he did not, he deserved, by that very fact, the death penalty. Compare to Leviticus
1.31: The Book of Proverbs concludes with an alphabetical piece, composed of as many verses or couplets as there are letters in the Hebrew alphabet, that is,
1.32: A general comparison between good and evil. This is the main subject of chapters
1.33: The Book of Proverbs concludes with three appendices (chapters
1.26: The Syrian hyrax. It resembles the rabbit, with which older versions commonly confused it. Hyraxes live in troops, in rock crevices, in Palestine. They are timid, due to the weakness of their legs. a people without strength , but they are wise, not straying far from their rocks, walking only with caution and fleeing as soon as they see one of the birds of prey that are hunting them.
1.5: that they do not make false decisions in the cause of the poor.
1.15: Roofs , etc. See the explanation of this verse, Proverbs
1.35: The depiction of drunkenness contained in these five verses is remarkably beautiful.
1.27: The grasshoppers… come out in groups often countless, and it is known that they sometimes completely devour crops.
1.14: His bread . We have already noted that the Hebrew term applies to all kinds of food.
1.23: At the gates of the city , where people gather and where justice is administered.
1.30: The meaning of this passage is that the wicked can only be healed or corrected by corporal punishments that are felt.
1.29: . Humiliation , etc.; a sentence often repeated in both the Old and New Testaments.
1.28: The lizard abounds in Palestine. "In the gorges that descend towards the Dead Sea, travelers have found [very large] lizards, including a species native to Egypt. Another species, called dhab , "It was found in the Jordan Valley, near the Quarantine Mountain; the Arabs eat it and use its skin to make sword sheaths, tobacco pouches, and also bags for storing butter." (Bishop Mislin.) Mosaic law classifies the lizard among unclean animals, see Leviticus ,

2.1: My son, if you receive my words and treasure my commandments within you,
2.2: making your ear attentive to wisdom and inclining your heart towards understanding,
2.3: Yes, if you call for prudence and raise your voice to intelligence,
2.4: If you search for it like money and dig into it as if to discover treasure,
2.5: Then you will understand the fear of the Lord and you will find the knowledge of God.
2.6: For the Lord gives wisdom; from his mouth come knowledge and understanding.,
2.7: He preserves happiness for the upright; he is a shield for those who walk in perfection.,
2.8: He protects the paths of justice, he watches over the way of his faithful.
2.9: Then you will understand justice, fairness, righteousness, and all the paths of goodness.
2.10: When wisdom enters your heart and knowledge delights your soul,
2.11: Reflection will watch over you and intelligence will protect you.,
2.12: to deliver you from the way of evil, from the man who speaks perverse words,
2.13: of those who abandon the straight paths, in order to walk in dark ways,
2.14: who rejoice in doing evil and find pleasure in the worst perversions,
2.15: whose paths are winding and follow oblique routes,
2.16: to deliver you from the woman who belongs to another, from the stranger who uses sweet words,
2.17: who abandons the companion of his youth and forgets the covenant of his God,
2.18: because she leans, along with her house, towards death, and her path leads to hell.,
2.19: Of all those who go to her, none return, none find their way back to the paths of life.
2.20: Thus you will walk in the way of good men and you will keep to the paths of the righteous.
2.21: For the upright will inhabit the earth, and the blameless will dwell there.,
2.22: But the wicked will be cut off from the earth, and the faithless will be uprooted from it.

3.1: My son, do not forget my teachings and let your heart keep my precepts.
3.2: They will bring you long days, years of life, and peace.
3.3: May mercy and truth not abandon you, fasten them around your neck, engrave them on the tablet of your heart.
3.4: In this way you will find favor and have true wisdom in the sight of God and men.
3.5: Trust in the Lord with all your heart and do not rely on your own understanding.
3.6: Remember him in all your ways and he will make your paths straight.
3.7: Do not be wise in your own eyes; fear the Lord and turn away from evil.
3.8: It will be good for your body and refreshing for your bones.
3.9: Honor God with your possessions, with the first fruits of all your income.
3.10: Then your barns will be filled to overflowing and your vats will overflow with new wine.
3.11: My son, do not despise the Lord's discipline, nor abhor his punishments.
3.12: For the Lord disciplines the one he loves, as a father disciplines the child he cherishes.
3.13: Blessed is the man who finds wisdom and the man who acquires understanding.
3.14: Acquiring it is better than acquiring silver, possessing it better than possessing pure gold.
3.15: It is more precious than pearls; all jewels cannot equal it.
3.16: In his right hand is long life, in his left, riches and glory.
3.17: His ways are pleasant ways, all his paths, paths of peace.
3.18: It is a tree of life for those who grasp it, and he who clings to it is happy.
3.19: It is by wisdom that the Lord founded the earth, by understanding that he established the heavens.
3.20: It is through his science that the abysses have opened and the clouds distill the dew.
3.21: My son, do not let them depart from your sight; keep wisdom and reflection.,
3.22: They will be the life of your soul and the ornament of your neck.
3.23: Then you will walk safely along your path and your foot will not stumble.
3.24: If you lie down, you will be without fear, and when you lie down, your sleep will be sweet.
3.25: You will have nothing to fear from sudden terror or an attack from the wicked.
3.26: For the Lord will be your assurance and he will keep your foot from every snare.
3.27: Do not refuse a benefit to those to whom it is due, when it is in your power to grant it.
3.28: Don't say to your neighbor, "Go and come back, tomorrow I'll give," when you can give right now.
3.29: Do not plot evil against your neighbor when he remains peaceful near you.
3.30: Don't argue with someone without reason, especially if they haven't done you any harm.
3.31: Do not envy the man violence and do not choose any of his paths:
3.32: for the Lord detests the perverse, but with the upright in heart is his intimacy.
3.33: The Lord's curse is on the house of the wicked, but he blesses the home of the righteous.
3.34: He mocks the mockers and gives grace to the humble.
3.35: Glory will be the portion of the wise, but shame will be the portion of the foolish.

4.1: Listen, my sons, to a father's instruction and pay attention, so that you may learn wisdom,
4.2: For I give you good teaching: do not abandon my teaching.
4.3: I too was a son to my father, a tender and only son to my mother.
4.4: He instructed me and said to me, «Let your heart retain my words, observe my precepts and you will live.
4.5: Acquire wisdom, acquire understanding, do not forget the words of my mouth and do not turn away from them.
4.6: Don't abandon her and she will keep you, love her and she will keep you.
4.7: This is the beginning of wisdom: Get wisdom, and out of all that you have, get understanding.
4.8: Hold her in high esteem and she will exalt you, she will bring you glory, if you embrace her.
4.9: She will place a crown of grace upon your head, she will adorn you with a magnificent diadem.»
4.10: Listen, my son, and receive my words, and the years of your life will multiply.
4.11: I will teach you the way of wisdom, I will lead you in the paths of righteousness.
4.12: If you walk, your steps will not be cramped, and if you run, you will not stumble.
4.13: Hold on to the instruction, don't abandon it, keep it because it is your life.
4.14: Do not enter the path of the wicked, nor walk in the way of evil men.
4.15: Avoid it, don't go through it, turn away from it and go on.
4.16: For they do not sleep unless they do evil; their sleep flees unless they cause someone to stumble.
4.17: For they eat the bread of crime, they drink the wine of violence.
4.18: The path of the righteous is like the bright morning light, which grows brighter and brighter until the day breaks.
4.19: The way of the wicked is like darkness; they do not see what will cause them to stumble.
4.20: My son, pay attention to my words, listen carefully to my speeches.
4.21: Do not let them depart from your sight, keep them in the midst of your heart.
4.22: For they are life to those who find them, health to their whole body.
4.23: Guard your heart above all else, for from it spring the fountains of life.
4.24: Keep deceitful speech out of your mouth and falsehood far from your lips.
4.25: Let your eyes look straight ahead and let your eyelids turn in front of you.
4.26: Make a level path for your feet, and let all your ways be straight.
4.27: Do not incline to the right or to the left, and turn your foot away from evil.

5.1: My son, pay attention to my wisdom and listen to my understanding,
5.2: so that you may retain understanding and your lips may preserve knowledge.
5.3: For the lips of the stranger distill honey and her palate is sweeter than oil.
5.4: But in the end it is bitter like absinthe, sharp like a double-edged sword.
5.5: His feet descend towards death, his steps go straight to Sheol.
5.6: She does not consider the path of life, her steps wander uncertainly, she does not know where.
5.7: And now, my sons, listen to me and do not turn away from the words of my mouth.
5.8: Keep your distance from her, do not approach the door of her house,
5.9: lest you surrender the flower of your youth and your years to others at the hands of a cruel tyrant,
5.10: lest strangers should become filled with your possessions and the fruit of your labor should pass into the house of another,
5.11: lest you should groan in the end, when your flesh and body are consumed,
5.12: and that you do not say: How then could I have hated discipline, and how could my heart have scorned reproof?
5.13: How could I have failed to listen to the voice of my teachers, failed to lend an ear to those who were instructing me?
5.14: I almost reached the height of misfortune, in the midst of the people and the assembly.
5.15: Drink the water from your cistern, the streams that flow from your well.
5.16: Let your springs overflow outside, let your streams flow in the public squares.
5.17: Let them be for you alone and not for strangers with you.
5.18: May your spring be blessed and find joy in the wife of your youth.
5.19: Charming doe, graceful gazelle, may her charms intoxicate you at all times, be always enamored of her love.
5.20: Why, my son, would you fall in love with a stranger and kiss the breast of an unknown woman?
5.21: For before the eyes of the Lord are the ways of man; he considers all his paths.
5.22: The wicked man is caught in his own iniquities; he is seized by the bonds of his sin.
5.23: He will die for lack of correction; he will be deceived by the excess of his madness.

6.1: My son, if you have stood surety for your friend, if you have given your word to a stranger,
6.2: If you are bound by the words of your mouth, if you are caught by the words of your mouth,
6.3: Do this, my friend: free yourself. Since you have fallen into the hands of your neighbor, go, prostrate yourself and press him hard.
6.4: Give neither sleep to your eyes, nor slumber to your eyelids,
6.5: Break free, like a gazelle from the hand of the hunter, like a bird from the hand of the fowler.
6.6: Go to the ant, O sluggard, consider its ways and become wise.
6.7: She who has neither chief, nor works inspector, nor sovereign,
6.8: It gathers its food in the summer, it collects its sustenance at harvest time.
6.9: How long, O sluggard, will you lie down? When will you get up from your sleep?
6.10: «"A little sleep, a little dozing, a little resting your hands on your bed."»
6.11: And your poverty will come like a traveler, and your want like an armed man.
6.12: A perverse man, an unjust man, walks with perversity in his mouth.,
6.13: He blinks his eyes, scratches his foot, and makes signs with his fingers.
6.14: Perversity is in his heart; he plots evil at all times; he stirs up quarrels.
6.15: Therefore, his downfall will come suddenly; he will be broken all at once and without remedy.
6.16: There are six things the Lord hates, seven things he abhors:
6.17: haughty eyes, a lying tongue, hands that spill innocent blood,
6.18: a heart that plots sinful schemes, feet eager to rush into evil,
6.19: the false witness who utters lies and the one who sows discord among brothers.
6.20: My son, keep your father's command and do not reject your mother's teaching.
6.21: Bind them constantly to your heart, fasten them around your neck.
6.22: He will guide you in your walk, he will watch over you in your sleep, and when you wake up, he will converse with you.
6.23: For the precept is a lamp and the law a light, and the warnings that instruct are the way of life.
6.24: They will protect you from the wicked woman, from the sweet tongue of the stranger.
6.25: Do not covet her beauty in your heart, and let her not seduce you with her eyelids.
6.26: Because for the courtesan one is reduced to a piece of bread, and the married woman traps a precious life.
6.27: Is it possible for a man to put fire in his bosom without his clothes catching fire?
6.28: Or shall a man walk on hot coals and not have his feet burned?
6.29: So it is with the one who goes to his neighbor's wife: whoever touches her will not go unpunished.
6.30: We do not despise a thief who steals to satisfy his hunger when he has nothing to eat:
6.31: Surprised, he gives back seven times as much, he gives everything he has in his house.
6.32: But he who corrupts a woman is senseless; he destroys himself; he who acts in this way,
6.33: He reaps only wounds and ignominy, and his disgrace will not be erased.
6.34: For jealousy stirs the fury of the wronged man; he is merciless on the day of vengeance.,
6.35: He doesn't care about any ransom, he doesn't want one, even if you were to multiply the gifts.

7.1: My son, remember my words and keep my precepts with you.
7.2: Observe my precepts and you will live; keep my teaching as the apple of your eye.
7.3: Link them on your fingers, write them on the table of your heart.
7.4: Say to wisdom: You are my sister, and call understanding your friend.,
7.5: so that she may protect you from another man's wife, from the stranger who whispers sweet words.
7.6: Standing at the window of my house, I was looking through the latticework.
7.7: Among the fools I saw, among the young men I noticed, a boy devoid of sense.
7.8: He was walking along the street, near the lodgings of a stranger, and he was approaching her house.
7.9: It was at dusk, at the fall of day, in the middle of the night and darkness.
7.10: And then a woman approaches him, dressed like a prostitute and with deceit in her heart.
7.11: She is impetuous and untamable; her feet cannot rest in her home.,
7.12: Sometimes in the street, sometimes in the squares and near all corners, she keeps watch.
7.13: She grabbed the young man and kissed him, and with a cheeky face said to him:
7.14: «"I was supposed to offer peaceful victims, today I have fulfilled my vows.".
7.15: That's why I went out to meet you, to look for you, and I found you.
7.16: I lined my bed with blankets and Egyptian cotton rugs.
7.17: I perfumed my bed with myrrh, aloe, and cinnamon.
7.18: Come, let us be intoxicated with love until morning, let us surrender to the delights of pleasure.
7.19: Because my husband is not at home, he has gone on a long trip.,
7.20: He took the bag of money with him; he won't return home until the full moon.»
7.21: She seduces him with her words, she entices him with the words on her lips.,
7.22: He immediately begins to follow her, like an ox going to the slaughter, like a fool running towards the punishment of fetters,
7.23: until an arrow pierces his liver, like a bird rushing into a net, unaware that its life is at stake.
7.24: And now, my sons, listen to me and pay attention to the words of my mouth.
7.25: Do not let your heart turn to his ways, do not stray into his paths.
7.26: Because it caused many injuries and many victims.
7.27: His house is the road to Sheol, which leads down to the abode of death.

8.1: Does not wisdom cry out, does not understanding raise her voice?
8.2: It is at the top of the heights, on the road, at the junction of paths, that she positions herself,
8.3: Near the gates, on the outskirts of the city, at the entrance to the gates, she makes her voice heard:
8.4: «"Men, it is to you that I cry out, and my voice is addressed to the children of men.".
8.5: Simple people, learn prudence; fools, learn intelligence.
8.6: Listen, for I have wonderful things to say, and my lips are open to teach what is right.
8.7: «For my mouth speaks the truth, and my lips abhor iniquity.”.
8.8: All the words of my mouth are just; there is nothing false or crooked in them.
8.9: All are just for the intelligent and upright for those who have found knowledge.
8.10: Receive my instruction rather than money, and knowledge rather than pure gold.
8.11: For wisdom is worth more than pearls, and the most precious objects cannot equal it.
8.12: «I, wisdom, dwell with prudence and possess the knowledge of reflection.
8.13: The fear of the Lord is hatred of evil, arrogance and pride, the way of evil and the perverse mouth, these are what I hate.
8.14: The advice and the success belong to me, I am the intelligence, the strength is mine.
8.15: Through me kings reign and princes decree what is just.
8.16: Through me rule the chiefs and the great men, all the judges of the earth.
8.17: I love those who love me, and those who earnestly seek me find me.
8.18: With me are riches and honor, lasting wealth and righteousness.
8.19: My fruit is better than gold, than pure gold, and what comes from me is better than refined silver.
8.20: I walk in the path of righteousness, in the midst of the paths of judgment,
8.21: to give possessions to those who love me and to fill their treasures.
8.22: «The Lord possessed me at the beginning of his ways, before his most ancient works.
8.23: I was founded from eternity, from the beginning, before the origins of the earth.
8.24: There were no deeps when I was born, no springs full of water.
8.25: Before the mountains were settled, before the hills, I was brought forth,
8.26: when he had not yet made the earth, nor the plains, nor the first elements of the dust of the globe.
8.27: When he established the heavens, I was there; when he drew a circle on the face of the deep,
8.28: when he established the clouds above and subdued the springs of the deep,
8.29: when he fixed its limit to the sea, so that the waters would not transgress its boundaries, when he laid the foundations of the earth.
8.30: I worked alongside him, rejoicing every day and constantly playing in his presence,
8.31: playing on the globe of his earth and finding my delights among the children of men.
8.32: «And now, my sons, listen to me: blessed are those who keep my ways.”.
8.33: Listen to instruction to become wise, do not reject it.
8.34: Blessed is the man who listens to me, who watches over my gates every day and guards their doorposts.
8.35: for whoever finds me finds life and obtains favor from the Lord.
8.36: But whoever offends me wounds his own soul, and all who hate me love death.»

9.1: Wisdom has built her house; she has hewn its seven pillars.
9.2: She immolated her victims, mixed her wine, and set her table.
9.3: She sent her servants, she called, from the top of the city's heights:
9.4: «"Let the simple one enter here." She said to the one who lacked sense:
9.5: «Come, eat my bread and drink the wine I have mixed,
9.6: Leave ignorance behind and you will live and walk in the path of intelligence.»
9.7: He who corrects the mocker incurs ridicule, and he who rebukes the wicked incurs insult.
9.8: Do not rebuke the mocker, lest he hate you; rebuke the wise and he will love you.
9.9: Give to the wise and he will become wiser, instruct the righteous and he will increase in knowledge.
9.10: The fear of the Lord is the beginning of wisdom, and understanding is the knowledge of the Holy One.
9.11: For through me your days will be multiplied, through me the years of your life will be increased.
9.12: If you are wise, you are wise to your own advantage; if you are mocking, you alone will bear the consequences.
9.13: Madness is a noisy, stupid woman who knows nothing.
9.14: She sat down on a seat at the door of her house, high above the city.,
9.15: to invite passers-by who are going straight on their way:
9.16: «"Let the simple one enter here." She said to the one who lacked sense:
9.17: «"Stolen waters are sweeter, and the bread of mystery is more pleasing."»
9.18: And he doesn't know that there are shadows there and that his guests are already in the depths of Sheol.

10.1: Parables of Solomon. The wise son brings joy to his father, but the foolish son brings grief to his mother.
10.2: Treasures acquired through crime do not bring profit, but justice delivers from death.
10.3: The Lord does not let the righteous go hungry, but he thwarts the craving of the wicked.
10.4: He who works with a lazy hand becomes poor, but the diligent hand amasses riches.
10.5: He who gathers during the summer is a prudent son, he who sleeps at harvest time is a son of shame.
10.6: Blessing rests upon the head of the righteous, but injustice covers the mouth of the wicked.
10.7: The memory of the righteous is a blessing, but the name of the wicked will rot.
10.8: The one who is wise in heart receives the precepts, but the one who is foolish in speech goes to his ruin.
10.9: He who walks in integrity walks in confidence, but he who takes crooked paths will be found out.
10.10: He who blinks his eyes will cause sorrow, and he who is foolish with his lips will come to ruin.
10.11: The mouth of the righteous is a fountain of life, but injustice covers the mouth of the wicked.
10.12: Hate stirs up quarrels, but love covers all faults.
10.13: Wisdom is found on the lips of the intelligent man, but the stick is for the back of the one who lacks sense.
10.14: The wise store up wisdom, but the mouth of a fool is a near disaster.
10.15: Fortune is the stronghold of the rich, the misfortune of the poor is their poverty.
10.16: The work of the righteous leads to life, but the gain of the wicked leads to sin.
10.17: He who heeds instruction takes the path of life, but he who forgets rebuke goes astray.
10.18: He who conceals hatred has lying lips, and he who spreads slander is a fool.
10.19: An abundance of words does not come without sin, but he who restrains his lips is a prudent man.
10.20: The tongue of the righteous is choice silver, the heart of the wicked is of little price.
10.21: The lips of the righteous feed many, but fools die for lack of understanding.
10.22: It is the blessing of the Lord that provides wealth, and the effort one takes adds nothing to it.
10.23: Committing a crime seems like a game to the fool, and the same is true of wisdom for the intelligent man.
10.24: What the wicked fear comes to him, and God grants the righteous what they desire.
10.25: As the whirlwind passes, the wicked disappear, the righteous are established on an eternal foundation.
10.26: What vinegar is to the teeth and smoke to the eyes, so is a lazy person to those who send him.
10.27: The fear of the Lord increases life, but the years of the wicked are shortened.
10.28: The expectation of the righteous brings joy, but the hope of the wicked will perish.
10.29: The way of the Lord is a stronghold for the righteous, but it is a ruin for those who do evil.
10.30: The righteous will never be shaken, but the wicked will not dwell on the earth.
10.31: The mouth of the righteous produces wisdom, and the perverse tongue will be torn out.
10.32: The lips of the righteous know grace, but the mouths of the wicked know perversity.

11.1: A false balance is an abomination to the Lord, but a just weight is his delight.
11.2: If pride comes, then disgrace will follow, but wisdom is with the humble.
11.3: The innocence of upright men guides them, but the deceitfulness of the treacherous ruins them.
11.4: In the day of wrath, riches profit nothing, but righteousness delivers from death.
11.5: The righteousness of the upright man guides his ways, but the wicked fall through their wickedness.
11.6: The justice of the upright delivers them, but the treacherous are caught by their own malice.
11.7: When the wicked man dies, his hope perishes and the expectation of the perverse man is annihilated.
11.8: The righteous are delivered from anguish, and the wicked fall into it in their place.
11.9: With his mouth the wicked prepare the ruin of his neighbor, but the righteous will be delivered by knowledge.
11.10: When the righteous are happy, the city rejoices; when the wicked perish, there are shouts of joy.
11.11: By the blessing of the upright the city prospers, it is overthrown by the mouth of the wicked.
11.12: He who despises his neighbor is senseless, but the intelligent man remains silent.
11.13: The slanderer reveals secrets, but the faithful man keeps hidden things.
11.14: When leadership is lacking, the people fall; salvation lies in a large number of advisors.
11.15: He who trusts a stranger will regret it, but he who fears to commit himself is safe.
11.16: The woman who has grace obtains glory, the energetic men acquire wealth.
11.17: A charitable man benefits his soul, but a cruel man harms his own flesh.
11.18: The wicked do deceptive work, but he who sows justice has a sure reward.
11.19: Justice leads to life, but he who pursues evil goes to death.
11.20: The Lord detests those with perverse hearts, but those who are blameless in their ways are pleasing to him.
11.21: No, the wicked will not go unpunished, but the posterity of the righteous will be saved.
11.22: A gold ring in a pig's nose, that is a beautiful woman devoid of sense.
11.23: The desire of the righteous is solely for good; the expectation of the wicked is fury.
11.24: One gives generously and gets rich, the other saves excessively and gets poorer.
11.25: The benevolent soul will be satisfied, and he who waters will himself be watered.
11.26: He who withholds the grain is cursed by the people, but the blessing is on the head of him who sells it.
11.27: He who seeks good finds favor, but he who seeks evil, evil will overtake him.
11.28: He who trusts in his riches will fall, but the righteous will flourish like leaves.
11.29: He who disturbs his house will inherit the wind, and the fool will be the slave of the wise man.
11.30: The fruit of the righteous is a tree of life, and he who wins souls is wise.
11.31: If the righteous receive punishment on earth, how much more so the wicked and the sinner.

12.1: He who loves instruction loves knowledge; he who hates reproof is a fool.
12.2: The one who is good obtains favor from the Lord, but the Lord condemns the wicked man.
12.3: Man is not established by wickedness, but the root of the righteous will not be shaken.
12.4: A virtuous woman is the crown of her husband, but a woman without honor is like decay in his bones.
12.5: The thoughts of the righteous are fairness, the counsels of the wicked are fraud.
12.6: The words of the wicked are deadly traps, but the mouth of the upright saves them.
12.7: The wicked man takes a turn and is no more, but the house of the righteous remains standing.
12.8: A man is esteemed according to his intelligence, but a man with a perverse heart will be despised.
12.9: Better is a humble man who provides for his own needs than a glorious man who lacks bread.
12.10: The righteous man takes care of his livestock, but the entrails of the wicked are cruel.
12.11: He who cultivates his field will have plenty of bread, but he who pursues vain things lacks sense.
12.12: The wicked covet the prey of the wicked, but the root of the righteous bears its fruit.
12.13: There is a deadly snare in the sin of the lips, but the righteous are delivered from distress.
12.14: It is by the fruit of his mouth that one is satisfied with good things, and it will be repaid to each according to the work of his hands.
12.15: The way of a fool is right in his own eyes, but the wise listen to advice.
12.16: The fool immediately shows his anger, but the prudent man knows how to conceal an insult.
12.17: He who proclaims the truth speaks what is right, and the false witness utters perfidy.
12.18: He who speaks rashly wounds like a sword, but the tongue of the wise brings healing.
12.19: The truthful language will always remain, but the lying language is confounded in the blink of an eye.
12.20: Fraud is in the hearts of those who plot evil, but joy belongs to those who advise peace.
12.21: No misfortune befalls the righteous, but the wicked are overwhelmed with evils.
12.22: Lying lips are an abomination to the Lord, but those who act according to the truth are pleasing to him.
12.23: A prudent man conceals his knowledge, but the heart of a fool proclaims his folly.
12.24: The vigilant hand will rule, but the indolent hand will be subservient.
12.25: Sorrow in a man's heart weighs him down, but a kind word cheers him up.
12.26: The righteous guide their friends, but the way of the wicked leads them astray.
12.27: The lazy man does not roast his game, but activity is a precious treasure for man.
12.28: In the path of justice lies life, and in the path it traces, immortality.

13.1: The wise son reveals his father's instruction, but the mocker does not heed the rebuke.
13.2: From the fruit of his mouth man tastes good, but the desire of the treacherous is violence.
13.3: He who guards his mouth preserves his soul, but he who opens his lips too wide will come to his ruin.
13.4: The lazy person has desires that are not satisfied, but the desires of the diligent will be fulfilled.
13.5: The righteous hate false speech, the wicked bring shame and confusion.
13.6: Justice preserves the path of the upright man, but wickedness brings ruin to the sinner.
13.7: The rich man who has nothing acts like this, and the poor man who has great possessions acts like this.
13.8: A man's wealth is the price of his life, but the poor man doesn't even hear the threat.
13.9: The light of the righteous shines brightly, but the lamp of the wicked is extinguished.
13.10: Pride only produces quarrels, but wisdom is with those who accept advice.
13.11: Ill-gotten wealth vanishes, but he who accumulates it little by little increases it.
13.12: Deferred hope makes the heart sick, but fulfilled desire is a tree of life.
13.13: He who despises the word will perish, but he who respects the precept will be rewarded.
13.14: The teachings of the wise are a source of life to escape the traps of death.
13.15: A cultivated intelligence produces grace, but the path of the treacherous is harsh.
13.16: Every prudent man acts with reflection, but a fool displays his folly.
13.17: A wicked messenger falls into misfortune, but a faithful messenger brings healing.
13.18: Misery and shame befall those who reject correction, while those who receive rebuke are honored.
13.19: Satisfied desire gladdens the soul, and turning away from evil horrifies fools.
13.20: He who walks with the wise becomes wise, but he who delights in the company of fools becomes wicked.
13.21: Misfortune pursues sinners, but happiness rewards the righteous.
13.22: A righteous man leaves his inheritance to his children's children, but the wealth of the sinner is reserved for the righteous.
13.23: In the field cleared by the poor, food abounds, but some perish for lack of justice.
13.24: He who spares his rod hates his son, but he who loves him corrects him early.
13.25: The righteous eat and satisfy their hunger, but the stomach of the wicked goes hungry.

14.1: The wise woman builds her house, but the foolish woman tears it down with her own hands.
14.2: He who fears the Lord walks in righteousness, and he who despises Him is perverse in his ways.
14.3: In the mouth of a fool is the staff of his pride, but the lips of the wise guard them.
14.4: Where there are no oxen, the manger is empty, but the strength of oxen provides abundant income.
14.5: A faithful witness does not lie, but a false witness utters lies.
14.6: The mocker seeks wisdom and does not find it, but for the intelligent man, knowledge is easy.
14.7: Stay away from the fool, for you know that knowledge is not on his lips.
14.8: The wisdom of the prudent man is to understand his way; the folly of fools is deceit.
14.9: The fool laughs at sin, but among the upright there is kindness.
14.10: The heart knows its own sorrows, and a stranger cannot share its joy.
14.11: The house of the wicked will be destroyed, but the tent of the righteous will flourish.
14.12: Such a path may seem right to a man, but its end is the path of death.
14.13: Even in laughter the heart finds pain, and joy ends in mourning.
14.14: The wicked will be filled with their ways, and the righteous with their fruits.
14.15: The simple man believes everything he says, but the prudent man watches his steps.
14.16: The wise man fears and turns away from evil, but the foolish man is enraged and remains safe.
14.17: A man who is quick to anger does foolish things, and a man who schemes attracts hatred.
14.18: The simple share in madness, and the prudent crown themselves with knowledge.
14.19: The wicked bow down before the good, and the wicked at the gates of the righteous.
14.20: The poor man is hateful even to his friend, but the rich man has many friends.
14.21: He who despises his neighbor commits a sin, but blessed is he who has pity on the unfortunate.
14.22: Do not those who plot evil go astray, while favor and truth belong to those who plot good?
14.23: All work produces abundance, but idle talk leads to poverty.
14.24: Wealth is a crown for the wise, but the folly of fools is still folly.
14.25: The truthful witness saves souls, the cunning one utters lies.
14.26: He who fears the Lord finds a strong refuge, and his children have a sure shelter.
14.27: The fear of the Lord is a source of life, to escape the snares of death.
14.28: A large population is the glory of the king, a lack of subjects is the ruin of the prince.
14.29: He who is slow to anger has great understanding, but he who is quick-tempered displays his folly.
14.30: A tranquil heart is the life of the body, but envy is the decay of the bones.
14.31: He who oppresses the poor insults his Maker, but he honors him who has pity on the needy.
14.32: The wicked are overthrown by their own malice, but even in death the righteous have faith.
14.33: Wisdom resides in the heart of the intelligent, and it is recognized even among fools.
14.34: Righteousness exalts a nation, but sin is a reproach to the peoples.
14.35: The king's favor is for the wise servant, and his anger is for the one who brings shame.

15.1: A gentle response calms fury, but harsh words stir up anger.
15.2: The tongue of the wise makes knowledge pleasing, but folly overflows from the mouth of fools.
15.3: The eyes of the Lord are everywhere, watching the wicked and the good.
15.4: Gentle speech is a tree of life, but a perverse tongue breaks the heart.
15.5: The fool despises his father's instruction, but he who heeds rebuke becomes wiser.
15.6: There is great wealth in the house of the righteous, but there is trouble in the gains of the wicked.
15.7: The lips of the wise spread knowledge, but not the heart of the fool.
15.8: The sacrifice of the wicked is an abomination to the Lord, but the prayer of the upright pleases him.
15.9: The way of the wicked is an abomination to the Lord, but he loves the one who pursues righteousness.
15.10: Severe punishment befalls those who abandon the path; those who hate rebuke will die.
15.11: Sheol and the abyss are uncovered before the Lord: how much more the hearts of the children of men.
15.12: The mocker does not like to be corrected, he does not go to the wise.
15.13: A joyful heart makes the face serene, but when the heart is sad, the spirit is downcast.
15.14: The intelligent heart seeks knowledge, but the mouth of fools feeds on folly.
15.15: All the days of the afflicted are bad, but a contented heart is a perpetual feast.
15.16: Better is a little with the fear of the Lord than great treasure with turmoil.
15.17: Vegetables with affection are better than a fat ox with hatred.
15.18: The violent man stirs up quarrels, but the patient man calms disputes.
15.19: The path of the lazy is like a hedge of thorns, but the path of the upright is smooth.
15.20: A wise son brings joy to his father, and a foolish son despises his mother.
15.21: Madness is a joy for the senseless man, but an intelligent man follows the right path.
15.22: Projects fail due to lack of deliberation, but they succeed when there are many advisors.
15.23: A man rejoices in a good answer from his mouth, and how pleasant is a word spoken at the right time.
15.24: The wise man follows a path of life that leads upwards, to turn away from Sheol which is below.
15.25: The Lord tears down the house of the proud, but he establishes the boundaries of the widow.
15.26: Evil thoughts are an abomination to the Lord, but kind words are pure in his sight.
15.27: He who is greedy for gain troubles his own household, but he who hates bribes will live.
15.28: The heart of the righteous considers what answer to give, but evil springs from the mouth of the wicked.
15.29: The Lord is far from the wicked, but he hears the prayer of the righteous.
15.30: A kind look gladdens the heart, good news strengthens the bones.
15.31: The ear that listens to salutary rebukes has its dwelling place among the wise.
15.32: He who rejects correction despises his own soul, but he who heeds reproof gains wisdom.
15.33: The fear of the Lord is the school of wisdom, and humility precedes glory.

16.1: Let man form plans in his heart, but the answer of the tongue comes from the Lord.
16.2: All the ways of man are pure in his eyes, but the Lord weighs the spirits.
16.3: Commit your works to the Lord and your plans will succeed.
16.4: The Lord has done everything for his purpose, and the wicked himself for the day of misfortune.
16.5: Anyone who is proud in heart is an abomination to the Lord; surely, he will not go unpunished.
16.6: Through kindness and faithfulness iniquity is atoned for, and through the fear of the Lord one turns away from evil.
16.7: When the Lord is pleased with a man's ways, he reconciles his enemies to himself.
16.8: Better to have little with justice than great income with injustice.
16.9: A man's heart plans his way, but it is the Lord who directs his steps.
16.10: The king has oracles on his lips, that his mouth should not sin when he judges.
16.11: The balance and the just scales are from the Lord, all the weights in the bag are his work.
16.12: It is an abomination for kings to do evil, for it is through justice that the throne is established.
16.13: Righteous lips enjoy the favor of kings, and they love him who speaks with integrity.
16.14: The king's fury is a messenger of death, but a wise man can calm it.
16.15: The serenity of the king's face gives life, and his favor is like spring rain.
16.16: Acquiring wisdom is far better than gold, acquiring intelligence is far preferable to silver.
16.17: The great path of righteous men is to avoid evil; he who watches over his way preserves his soul.
16.18: Pride goes before ruin, and arrogance before a fall.
16.19: It is better to be humble with the lowly than to share the spoils with the proud.
16.20: He who heeds the word finds happiness, and he who trusts in the Lord is blessed.
16.21: He who is wise of heart is called intelligent, and the sweetness of speech increases knowledge.
16.22: Wisdom is a source of life for the one who possesses it, and the punishment of the fool is his folly.
16.23: The heart of the wise gives wisdom to his mouth and increases knowledge on his lips.
16.24: Kind words are like a honeycomb, sweet to the soul and beneficial to the body.
16.25: Such a path may seem right to a man, but its end is the path of death.
16.26: The worker works for him, because his mouth excites him to do so.
16.27: The wicked man plots misfortune, and there is something like a burning fire on his lips.
16.28: The perverse man stirs up quarrels and the tattletale divides friends.
16.29: The violent man seduces his neighbor and leads him down a path that is not good.
16.30: He who closes his eyes to meditate on deception, he who purses his lips, already commits evil.
16.31: White hair is a crown of honor; it is found in the path of justice.
16.32: He who is slow to anger is better than a hero, and he who rules his mind than a warrior who takes cities.
16.33: Lots are cast into the folds of the robe, but every decision comes from the Lord.

17.1: Better a piece of dry bread with peace than a house full of meat with discord.
17.2: A prudent servant prevails over a shameful son, and he will share the inheritance with the brothers.
17.3: The crucible tests silver and the furnace gold, but the Lord tests hearts.
17.4: The wicked listen to the unjust lip, the liar lends an ear to the evil tongue.
17.5: He who mocks the poor insults his Maker; he who rejoices in misfortune will not go unpunished.
17.6: Children's children are the crown of the aged, and fathers are the glory of their children.
17.7: Distinguished words are not fitting for a fool, but lying words are even less fitting for a nobleman.
17.8: A gift is a precious stone in the eyes of the one who possesses it; wherever he turns, he finds success.
17.9: He who covers up a fault seeks friendship, and he who brings it up in his words divides friends.
17.10: One rebuke makes more of an impression on an intelligent man than a hundred blows on a fool.
17.11: The villain seeks only rebellion, but a cruel messenger will be sent against him.
17.12: It is better to encounter a bear deprived of her cubs than a madman in the throes of his madness.
17.13: He who returns evil for good will never see misfortune leave his house.
17.14: Starting a quarrel is like opening a dam; before the argument ignites, withdraw.
17.15: He who absolves the guilty and he who condemns the righteous are both an abomination to the Lord.
17.16: What good is money in the hand of a fool? To buy wisdom? He has no sense for that.
17.17: A friend loves at all times; in misfortune, he becomes a brother.
17.18: The unintelligent man makes commitments; he acts as guarantor for his fellow man.
17.19: He who loves quarrels loves sin, and he who exults in speech loves his own destruction.
17.20: He who has a false heart will not find happiness, and he who has a perverse tongue will fall into misfortune.
17.21: He who gives birth to a fool will have sorrow, the father of a madman will not be joyous.
17.22: A joyful heart is excellent medicine; a broken spirit dries up the bones.
17.23: The wicked receive gifts hidden in the fold of the cloak, to pervert the paths of justice.
17.24: The wise man has wisdom before him, but the eyes of the fool are at the ends of the earth.
17.25: A foolish son brings grief to his father and bitterness to his mother.
17.26: It is not good to fine the righteous, nor to condemn nobles because of their righteousness.
17.27: He who restrains his words possesses knowledge, and he who is calm of mind is a man of intelligence.
17.28: Even a fool, when he keeps silent, is considered wise; when he closes his lips, he is considered intelligent.

18.1: He who keeps himself apart seeks only to satisfy his passion; he becomes irritated by all wise advice.
18.2: It is not intelligence that pleases the fool, but the expression of his thoughts.
18.3: When evil comes, so does contempt, and with shame comes disgrace.
18.4: The words of man's mouth are deep waters, the fountain of wisdom is an overflowing torrent.
18.5: It is not good to show partiality to the wicked, so as to wrong the righteous in judgment.
18.6: The lips of the fool stir up strife, and his mouth provokes outrage.
18.7: The mouth of a fool brings about his ruin, and his lips are a snare to his soul.
18.8: The rapporteur's words are like tasty morsels; they go right down to the very depths of the soul.
18.9: He who is cowardly in his work is a brother to him who is headed for perdition.
18.10: The name of the Lord is a strong tower; the righteous run to it and are safe.
18.11: The rich man's fortune is his fortified city; in his mind, it is a high wall.
18.12: Before ruin, the heart of man is proud, but humility precedes glory.
18.13: He who answers before listening, that is madness and confusion for him.
18.14: A man's spirit sustains him in sickness, but a broken spirit, who will lift him up?
18.15: An intelligent heart acquires knowledge, and the ear of the wise seeks knowledge.
18.16: A man's gift broadens his path and introduces him to the powerful.
18.17: The first party to present their case appears to be just, then the opposing party comes forward and the dispute is examined.
18.18: Fate puts an end to disputes and decides between the powerful.
18.19: A brother who is an enemy of his brother is more resilient than a fortified city, and their quarrels are like the bolts of a palace.
18.20: It is from the fruit of a man's mouth that his body is nourished, and from the produce of his lips that he is satisfied.
18.21: Death and life are in the power of the tongue; according to its choice, one will eat its fruits.
18.22: He who finds a wife finds happiness; it is a favor that he has received from the Lord.
18.23: The poor man speaks in supplication and the rich man responds harshly.
18.24: A man with many friends may have them to his downfall, but there is one friend more devoted than a brother.

19.1: Better is the poor man who walks in his integrity than the man with disdainful lips who is foolish.
19.2: Ignorance of the soul is not good, and he whose feet are hasty falls.
19.3: A man's folly perverts his way, and his heart rages against the Lord.
19.4: Wealth brings many friends, but the poor man is separated from his friend.
19.5: The false witness will not go unpunished, and the one who tells lies will not escape.
19.6: Many are the flatterers of the generous man, and all are friends of the one who gives gifts.
19.7: All the poor man's brothers hate him, how much more do his friends distance themselves from him. He seeks kind words and finds none.
19.8: He who acquires intelligence loves his soul, and he who observes prudence will attain happiness.
19.9: The false witness will not go unpunished, and he who tells lies will perish.
19.10: It is not fitting for a fool to live in luxury, much less for a slave to rule over princes.
19.11: A man's wisdom makes him patient, and he prides himself on forgetting offenses.
19.12: The king's anger is like the roar of a lion, and his favor is like dew on the grass.
19.13: A foolish son is a father's misfortune, and a wife's quarrels are an endless gutter.
19.14: A house and wealth are a paternal inheritance, but an intelligent wife is a gift from the Lord.
19.15: Laziness leads to drowsiness, and a listless soul will experience hunger.
19.16: He who keeps the commandment keeps his soul; he who does not heed his way will die.
19.17: He who is kind to the poor lends to the Lord, who will reward his good deed.
19.18: Discipline your son, for there is still hope, but do not go so far as to put him to death.
19.19: The man with a violent temper will suffer the consequences; if you save him once, you will have to do it again.
19.20: Listen to advice and receive instruction, so that you may be wise in the rest of your life.
19.21: Many plans stir in the heart of man, but it is the Lord's purpose that is fulfilled.
19.22: What recommends a man is his goodness, and a poor man is better than a liar.
19.23: The fear of the Lord leads to life and one remains satisfied, without being visited by misfortune.
19.24: The lazy man dips his hand into the dish and does not bring it back to his mouth.
19.25: Strike the mocker and the simple man will become wise, rebuke the intelligent man and he will understand knowledge.
19.26: He who mistreats his father and drives away his mother is a son who covers himself with shame and disgrace.
19.27: Cease, my son, to listen to instruction and you will stray from the words of science.
19.28: A perverse witness mocks justice, and the mouths of the wicked swallow iniquity.
19.29: Judgments are ready for the mockers and blows for the backs of the fools.

20.1: Wine is a mocker, fermented drinks encourage rowdiness, whoever indulges in them is not wise.
20.2: Like the roar of a lion is the terror inspired by the king; he who angers him sins against himself.
20.3: It is to a man's glory to abstain from quarrels, but every fool gives himself over to anger.
20.4: Because of the bad weather, the lazy man does not plow; at harvest time he will search and find nothing.
20.5: The thought in the heart of man is a deep water, but the intelligent man will draw from it.
20.6: Many men boast of their goodness, but a faithful man, who can find?
20.7: The righteous man walks in his integrity, and blessed are his children after him.
20.8: The king, seated on the throne of justice, dispels all evil with his gaze.
20.9: Who will say, «I have purified my heart; I am clean from my sin»?»
20.10: Weight and weight, ephah and ephah, are both an abomination to the Lord.
20.11: The child already shows by his actions whether his works will be pure and righteous.
20.12: The hearing ear and the seeing eye, the Lord made both.
20.13: Do not love sleep, lest you become poor; open your eyes and fill yourself with bread.
20.14: "Bad, bad," said the buyer, and as he left, he congratulated himself.
20.15: There is gold and many pearls, but wise lips are a precious vessel.
20.16: Take his garment, for he has answered for another; demand pledges from him because of the foreigners.
20.17: The bread of deceit is sweet to man, but in the end his mouth is filled with gravel.
20.18: Projects are strengthened by advice; conduct war with prudence.
20.19: He who goes about slandering reveals secrets, carefully avoids him whose lips are always open.
20.20: If someone curses his father and mother, his lamp will be extinguished in the darkness.
20.21: An inheritance that one rushes to acquire at first will not be blessed in the end.
20.22: Do not say, "I will repay evil," hope in the Lord and he will deliver you.
20.23: Weight and scales are an abomination to the Lord, and false balances are not a good thing.
20.24: It is the Lord who directs man's steps, and can man understand his way?
20.25: It is a trap for man to say lightly, "This is sacred," and only reflect after the vow has been made.
20.26: A wise king disperses the wicked and makes the wheel pass over them.
20.27: The soul of man is a lamp of the Lord, it penetrates to the depths of the bowels.
20.28: Kindness and loyalty protect the king, and he strengthens his throne through kindness.
20.29: Strength is the adornment of young men, and white hair is the ornament of old men.
20.30: The bruise that tears the flesh heals the disease, as do the blows that reach deep within the entrails.

21.1: The king's heart is a stream in the Lord's hand; he inclines it wherever he wills.
21.2: All of a man's ways are right in his eyes, but the Lord weighs hearts.
21.3: Practicing justice and fairness is preferable in the eyes of the Lord to sacrifices.
21.4: Haughty looks and a proud heart: a torch for the wicked, that is nothing but sin.
21.5: The plans of the diligent man lead only to abundance, but whoever rushes his steps only arrives at scarcity.
21.6: Treasures acquired through a lying tongue: the fleeting vanity of men rushing towards their deaths.
21.7: The violence of the wicked leads them astray, because they have refused to practice justice.
21.8: The path of the criminal is crooked, but the innocent act with integrity.
21.9: It is better to live on a rooftop than to stay with a quarrelsome wife.
21.10: The soul of the wicked desires evil; his friend finds no favor in his eyes.
21.11: When the wicked are punished, the simple become wise, and when the wise are instructed, they become wiser.
21.12: The righteous man regards the house of the wicked; God thrusts the wicked into misfortune.
21.13: He who closes his ear to the cry of the poor will himself cry out without anyone answering him.
21.14: A gift given in secret soothes anger, a present taken from the fold of one's cloak calms violent fury.
21.15: It is a joy for the righteous to practice justice, but terror for those who do evil.
21.16: The man who deviates from the path of prudence will rest in the assembly of the dead.
21.17: He who loves pleasure will be poor, he who loves wine and perfumed oil will not become rich.
21.18: The wicked serve as a ransom for the righteous, and the treacherous for the upright.
21.19: It is better to live in a deserted land than with a quarrelsome and angry wife.
21.20: Precious treasures and oil are in the house of the wise, but a foolish man squanders them.
21.21: He who pursues justice and mercy will find life, justice, and glory.
21.22: The wise man storms a city of heroes and he overturns the wall in which it placed its trust.
21.23: He who guards his mouth and his tongue keeps his soul from anguish.
21.24: A mocker is a haughty man, puffed up with pride, who acts with excessive arrogance.
21.25: The desires of the lazy man kill him, because his hands refuse to work.
21.26: All day long he desires ardently, but the righteous give without ceasing.
21.27: The sacrifice of the wicked is abominable, especially when they offer it with criminal thoughts.
21.28: The lying witness will perish, but the man who listens will always be able to speak.
21.29: The villain puts on a brazen air, but the righteous man steers his own course.
21.30: There is no wisdom, no prudence, no counsel in the face of the Lord.
21.31: The horse is equipped for the day of battle, but victory depends on the Lord.

22.1: A good reputation is better than great riches, and esteem is more precious than silver and gold.
22.2: The rich and the poor meet; the Lord is their author.
22.3: The prudent man sees the evil and hides, but the simple ones ignore it and bear the punishment.
22.4: The fruit of humility is the fear of the Lord, which is riches, glory, and life.
22.5: Thorns and snares lie in the path of the wicked, but he who guards his soul stays away from them.
22.6: Train up a child in the way he should go, and even when he is old he will not depart from it.
22.7: The rich rule over the poor, and the borrower is the slave of the lender.
22.8: He who sows injustice reaps misfortune, and the staff of his anger disappears.
22.9: The man with a kind gaze will be blessed, because he gives his bread to the poor.
22.10: Drive away the mocker and the quarrel will end, the dispute and the insult will cease.
22.11: He who loves purity of heart and has grace on his lips, has the king as his friend.
22.12: The eyes of the Lord preserve knowledge, but he confounds the words of the perverse.
22.13: The lazy man said, "There's a lion outside. I'll be killed in the middle of the public squares."«
22.14: The mouth of adulterous women is a deep pit; he against whom the Lord is angry will fall into it.
22.15: Madness is attached to the heart of the child; the stick of discipline will drive it away from him.
22.16: To oppress a poor person is to enrich them, to give to a rich person is to impoverish them.
22.17: Lend an ear and listen to the words of the wise, apply your heart to my teaching.
22.18: For it is a pleasant thing, if you keep them within you: may they all remain on your lips.
22.19: So that your trust may rest on the Lord, it is you whom I want to instruct today.
22.20: Have I not, several times already, put advice and teachings in writing for you?
22.21: To teach you the truth about certain things, so that you may answer with true words to those who send you.
22.22: Do not rob the poor because they are poor, and do not oppress the unfortunate at the gate.
22.23: For the Lord will take up their cause and he will take the lives of those who have plundered them.
22.24: Do not associate with an angry man, and do not go with a violent man.,
22.25: lest you learn his ways and set a snare for your soul.
22.26: Do not be one of those who make commitments, one of those who stand surety for debts.
22.27: If you can't afford to pay, why risk having your bed removed from under you?
22.28: Do not move the ancient boundary stone that your ancestors placed there.
22.29: Do you see a man skilled in his work? He will dwell with kings, he will not dwell with obscure people.

23.1: If you are at a table with an adult, pay attention to what is in front of you.
23.2: Put a knife to your throat if you're too greedy.
23.3: Do not covet its delicate dishes, it is a deceptive food.
23.4: Don't torment yourself trying to become rich; refrain from applying your intelligence to it.
23.5: Do you want to pursue with your eyes that which is about to vanish? For wealth grows wings and, like an eagle, flies to the heavens.
23.6: Do not eat the bread of the envious man, nor covet his delicacies.,
23.7: for he is worth no more than the thoughts of his soul. "Eat and drink," he will tell you, but his heart is not with you.
23.8: You'll vomit up the piece you ate and you'll pay for your fine words.
23.9: Do not speak in the ears of a fool, for he will despise the wisdom of your words.
23.10: Do not move the ancient boundary stone and do not enter the field of the orphans.
23.11: For their avenger is powerful: he will defend their cause against you.
23.12: Apply your heart to instruction and your ears to the words of knowledge.
23.13: Don't spare the child from punishment; if you hit him with the stick, he won't die.
23.14: You strike him with the staff and you deliver his soul from Sheol.
23.15: My son, if your heart is wise, my heart will also rejoice.
23.16: My heart will leap for joy when your lips speak what is right.
23.17: Let not your heart envy sinners, but let it always remain in the fear of the Lord.,
23.18: because there is a future and your hope will not be destroyed.
23.19: Listen, my son, and be wise; direct your heart in the right way.
23.20: Do not be among wine drinkers, among those who gorge themselves on meat,
23.21: because the drinker and the glutton become poor, and drowsiness makes one wear rags.
23.22: Listen to your father, who gave you life, and do not despise your mother when she is old.
23.23: Acquire the truth and do not sell it, wisdom, instruction, and understanding.
23.24: The father of the righteous rejoices, and he who gives birth to a wise child will be glad.
23.25: May your father and mother rejoice. May she who gave birth to you be glad.
23.26: My son, give me your heart and let your eyes guard my ways:
23.27: because the courtesan is a deep pit and the foreign woman a narrow well.
23.28: She sets traps as if for prey and increases the number of wrongdoers among men.
23.29: Who gets the "ahs"? Who gets the "alas"? Who gets the arguments? Who gets the whispers? Who gets the senseless hurts? Who gets the red eyes?
23.30: For those who linger over wine, for those who will taste flavored wine.
23.31: Do not look at the wine: how crimson it is, how brilliant it is in the cup, how smoothly it flows.
23.32: It ends up biting like a snake and stinging like a basilisk.
23.33: Your eyes will be fixed on foreign women, and your heart will utter perverse words.
23.34: You will be like a man lying in the middle of the sea, like a man asleep at the top of a mast.
23.35: «"I was hit. I'm not hurt. I was beaten. I feel nothing. When will I wake up? I need more."»

24.1: It does not envy wicked men and does not desire to be with them.
24.2: For their hearts plot violence and their lips utter only misfortune.
24.3: It is through wisdom that a house is built, and through understanding that it is established.
24.4: It is through science that the interior is filled with all precious and pleasant goods.
24.5: A wise man is full of strength, and one who has knowledge shows great power.
24.6: For with prudence you will conduct the war, and salvation lies in the abundance of counselors.
24.7: Wisdom is too high for a fool; he does not open his mouth at the city gate.
24.8: He who thinks of doing evil is called a craftsman of intrigue.
24.9: The plan of the fool is sin, and the mocker is an abomination among men.
24.10: If you show yourself weak in the day of distress, your strength is but weakness.
24.11: Deliver those being dragged to their deaths, those who are staggering to the slaughter, save them.
24.12: If you say, "But we did not know," does not He who weighs hearts see it? Does not He who watches over your soul know it and will He repay each person according to their deeds?
24.13: My son, eat honey, for it is good; a honeycomb is sweet to your palate.
24.14: Know that wisdom is the same for your soul; if you acquire it, there is a future and your hope will not be frustrated.
24.15: Do not, O wicked one, lay snares for the dwelling of the righteous, nor devastate the place where he rests.,
24.16: For the righteous fall seven times and rise again, but the wicked are plunged into calamity.
24.17: If your enemy falls, do not rejoice, nor let your heart rejoice in his ruin.,
24.18: lest the Lord see it, and it be evil in his sight, and turn away his anger from him.
24.19: Don't be angered by the wicked, don't envy the perverse,
24.20: because there is no future for the one who does evil, and the lamp of the wicked will be extinguished.
24.21: My son, fear the Lord and the king, do not associate with those who want to change everything,
24.22: For suddenly their misfortune will arise, and who knows the ruin of either side?
24.23: The following also comes from the wise: It is not good to show partiality in judgments.
24.24: He who says to the wicked, "You are righteous," the peoples curse him, the nations abhor him.
24.25: But those who correct it are applauded; blessings and happiness come upon them.
24.26: He kisses on the lips the one who responds with true words.
24.27: Order your work outside, apply it to your field, then you will build your house.
24.28: Do not bear light witness against your neighbor: would you deceive with your lips?
24.29: Do not say, "As he has done to me, I will do to him; I will repay him according to his deeds."«
24.30: I passed by the field of a sluggard and by the vineyard of a fool.
24.31: And behold, … thorns grew everywhere, brambles covered the surface, and the stone wall had collapsed.
24.32: I watched and I applied my heart, I considered and I drew this lesson:
24.33: «"A little sleep, a little dozing, a little folding of the hands to sleep,
24.34: And your poverty will come like a prowler, and your want like an armed man.»

25.1: Here are more Proverbs of Solomon, collected by the people of Hezekiah, king of Judah.
25.2: The glory of God is to conceal things, the glory of kings is to examine them.
25.3: Heaven in its height, earth in its depth, and the hearts of kings are unfathomable.
25.4: Remove the dross from the silver and a vessel will come out for the refiner.
25.5: Remove the wicked from before the king, and his throne will be established in righteousness.
25.6: Don't put on airs in front of the king and don't try to act like you're in the place of the powerful.,
25.7: for it is better that they tell you, "Come up here," than that they humiliate you before the prince whom your eyes have seen.
25.8: Don't rush into protesting, lest in the end you not know what to do.
25.9: When your neighbor has wronged you, defend yourself against your neighbor, but do not reveal another's secret.,
25.10: lest whoever hears of it should shame you and your disgrace should not be erased.
25.11: Like golden apples in silver settings, so is a word spoken at the right time.
25.12: Like a gold ring and an ornament of fine gold, so is the wise man who takes back a docile ear.
25.13: Like the freshness of snow at harvest time, so is the faithful messenger to those who send him; he gladdens the soul of his master.
25.14: Clouds and wind without rain, such is the man who boasts of deceitful gifts.
25.15: With patience the judge can be persuaded, and a gentle tongue can break bones.
25.16: If you find honey, eat only what you need, lest you vomit it up when you are full.
25.17: Rarely set foot in your neighbor's house, lest, tired of you, he may hate you.
25.18: A club, a sword and a sharp arrow, such is the man who bears false witness against his neighbor.
25.19: A broken tooth and a slipping foot, that's the confidence a treacherous person inspires on a day of misfortune.
25.20: Taking off one's coat on a cold day, pouring vinegar on a wound, so does one who sings songs to an afflicted heart.
25.21: If your enemy is hungry, give him bread to eat; if he is thirsty, give him water to drink.,
25.22: because you are heaping coals on his head, and the Lord will reward you.
25.23: The north wind brings forth rain and a tongue that meditates in secret, an irritated face.
25.24: It is better to live on a rooftop than to stay with a quarrelsome wife.
25.25: Fresh water for a thirsty person, that is good news from a distant land.
25.26: A troubled fountain and a corrupt spring, such is the righteous man who stumbles before the wicked.
25.27: It is not good to eat too much honey, so that whoever wants to fathom divine majesty will be overwhelmed by its glory.
25.28: A city forced open without walls, such is the man who cannot contain himself.

26.1: Like snow in summer and rain during harvest, so glory is not fitting for a fool.
26.2: Like the sparrow that escapes, like the swallow that flies away, so the curse without cause does not reach.
26.3: The whip is for the horse, the bit for the donkey, and the stick for the backs of fools.
26.4: Do not answer a fool according to his folly, lest you yourself become like him.
26.5: Answer a fool according to his folly, lest he consider himself wise.
26.6: He cuts off his feet, he drinks iniquity, he who gives messages to a fool.
26.7: "Take off the legs of the lame man," is the saying from the mouth of the fool.
26.8: It is like attaching a stone to a slingshot to give glory to a fool.
26.9: Like a thorn that pierces the hand of a drunkard, so is a saying in the mouth of fools.
26.10: Like an archer who wounds everyone, so is he who hires fools and passersby.
26.11: Like a dog that returns to its vomit, so is a fool who returns to his folly.
26.12: If you see a man who is wise in his own eyes, you should have more hope for a fool than for him.
26.13: The lazy man said, "There's a lion on the road, there's a lion in the streets."«
26.14: The door turns on its hinges, so does the lazy man on his bed.
26.15: The lazy man puts his hand in the dish and has difficulty bringing it to his mouth.
26.16: In his eyes, the lazy man is wiser than seven prudent advisors.
26.17: Like someone who grabs a dog by the ears, so is the passerby who gets worked up in another's quarrel.
26.18: Like a madman hurling flaming darts, arrows, and death,
26.19: Such is a man who has deceived his neighbor and says, "Was I not joking?"«
26.20: For lack of wood, the fire goes out; remove the informer and the quarrel subsides.
26.21: Coal makes a blaze and wood makes fire: thus a quarrelsome man irritates a discussion.
26.22: The words of the rapporteur are like sweets, they go down to the very depths of the soul.
26.23: Silver dross applied to an earthen vessel is like burning lips with an evil heart.
26.24: He who hates disguises himself with his lips, but he harbors treachery within himself.
26.25: When he softens his voice, do not trust him, for he has seven abominations in his heart.
26.26: He may well hide his hatred under dissimulation, but his wickedness will be revealed in the assembly.
26.27: He who digs a pit falls into it, and the stone rolls back on him who rolls it.
26.28: A false tongue hates those it hurts, and a flattering mouth causes ruin.

27.1: Do not boast about tomorrow, for you do not know what the following day will bring.
27.2: Let another praise you and not your own mouth, a stranger and not your own lips.
27.3: The stone is heavy and the sand is weighty: more than either of them weighs the anger of the fool.
27.4: Fury is cruel and anger impetuous, but who can stand against jealousy?
27.5: An open reprimand is better than a hidden friendship.
27.6: A friend's wounds are inspired by loyalty, but an enemy's kisses are deceitful.
27.7: He who is full tramples the honeycomb underfoot, but to him who is hungry everything bitter seems sweet.
27.8: Like the bird that wanders far from its nest, so is the man who wanders far from his home.
27.9: Oil and perfumes gladden the heart, like the sweetness of a friend whose advice comes from the heart.
27.10: Do not forsake your friend and your father’s friend, and do not enter your brother’s house in the day of your distress; better is a neighbor who is near than a brother who is far away.
27.11: My son, become wise and gladden my heart, so that I may answer him who insults me.
27.12: The prudent man sees evil and hides, the simple pass by and bear the punishment.
27.13: If someone stands surety for a stranger's debt, demand his garment; if he stands surety for a foreigner, demand pledges.
27.14: Blessing one's neighbor aloud and early in the morning is considered a curse.
27.15: A continuous gutter on a rainy day and a quarrelsome woman are alike.
27.16: He who holds it back holds back the wind, and his hand grasps oil.
27.17: Iron sharpens iron, and so one man sharpens another.
27.18: He who looks after his fig tree will eat its fruit, and he who looks after his master will be honored.
27.19: Just as in water face responds to face, so the heart of man responds to man.
27.20: Sheol and the abyss are never satisfied, nor are the eyes of man ever satisfied.
27.21: The crucible is for silver and the furnace for gold; let man likewise test the praise which he receives.
27.22: Even if you were to pound the fool in a mortar, as one grinds grain with a pestle, his madness would not separate from him.
27.23: Know the condition of your sheep well, pay close attention to your flock,
27.24: because wealth does not last forever, nor does a crown last from age to age.
27.25: But when the grass appeared, when the greenery showed itself, when the mountain hay was gathered,
27.26: You have lambs to clothe you, goats to pay for a field,
27.27: You have plenty of goat's milk, for your food and for your household, and for the care of your servants.

28.1: The wicked flee when no one pursues them, but the righteous are as bold as a lion.
28.2: In a country in revolt, leaders multiply, but with an intelligent and wise man, order endures.
28.3: A poor man who oppresses the unfortunate is like a violent rain that causes famine.
28.4: Those who abandon the law praise the wicked, while those who observe it are angry with them.
28.5: Wicked men do not understand what is right, but those who seek the Lord understand everything.
28.6: Better is a poor man in his integrity than a man of crooked ways who is rich.
28.7: He who observes the law is a wise son, but he who feeds debauchers brings shame upon his father.
28.8: He who increases his wealth by interest and usury amasses it for him who has compassion on the poor.
28.9: If anyone turns a deaf ear to the law, even their prayer is an abomination.
28.10: He who leads the upright astray into evil will himself fall into the pit he has dug, but the blameless will possess happiness.
28.11: The rich man is wise in his eyes, but the intelligent poor man knows it.
28.12: When the righteous triumph, there is a great celebration; when the wicked rise up, everyone hides.
28.13: He who conceals his faults will not prosper, but he who confesses them and abandons them will obtain mercy.
28.14: Blessed is the man who always fears the Lord, but he who hardens his heart will fall into misfortune.
28.15: A roaring lion and a hungry bear, such is the wicked one who rules over a poor people.
28.16: The prince without intelligence multiplies oppression, but he who hates greed will have long days.
28.17: A man covered in another's blood flees to the grave: do not stop him.
28.18: He who walks in integrity will find salvation, but he who follows crooked paths will fall, never to rise again.
28.19: He who cultivates his field will be filled with bread, but he who pursues vain things will be filled with poverty.
28.20: A faithful man will be filled with blessings, but one who is eager to get rich will not escape sin.
28.21: It is not good to show favoritism between people; for a piece of bread, a man becomes a criminal.
28.22: The envious man is eager to get rich, he does not know that famine will come upon him.
28.23: He who corrects someone finds more favor than he who flatters with his tongue.
28.24: He who steals from his father and mother and says, "It is not a sin," is a companion of a robber.
28.25: A greedy man stirs up quarrels, but he who trusts in the Lord will be satisfied.
28.26: He who trusts in his own heart is a fool, but he who walks in wisdom will be saved.
28.27: He who gives to the poor will not go hungry, but he who closes his eyes will be burdened with curses.
28.28: When the wicked rise up, everyone hides; when they perish, the righteous multiply.

29.1: The blameworthy man who stiffens his neck will be broken suddenly and without remedy.
29.2: When the righteous multiply, the people rejoice; when the wicked exercise power, the people groan.
29.3: A man who loves wisdom makes his father glad, but a man who consorts with prostitutes squanders his wealth.
29.4: A king establishes the country through justice, but one who is greedy for gifts ruins it.
29.5: The man who flatters his neighbor spreads a net beneath his feet.
29.6: In the sin of the wicked man there is a snare, but the righteous man is in jubilation and joy.
29.7: The righteous man knows the cause of the poor, but the wicked man does not understand science.
29.8: The mockers fan the flames in the city, but the wise calm the anger.
29.9: If a wise man argues with a fool, whether he gets angry or laughs, there will be no peace.
29.10: Bloodthirsty men hate the upright man, but righteous men protect his life.
29.11: The fool gives free rein to his passion, but the wise man calms and restrains it.
29.12: When the prince listens to lying words, all his servants are wicked.
29.13: The poor man and the oppressor meet, and it is the Lord who enlightens the eyes of both.
29.14: A king who faithfully judges the poor will have his throne secured forever.
29.15: The stick and correction give wisdom, but a child left to his whim brings shame to his mother.
29.16: When the wicked multiply, crime multiplies, but the righteous will witness their downfall.
29.17: Discipline your son and he will give you rest and bring delight to your soul.
29.18: When there is no vision, the people are unrestrained; happy is he who observes the law.
29.19: You cannot correct a slave with words; even if he understands, he does not obey.
29.20: If you see a man quick to speak, there is more to be hoped for from a fool than from him.
29.21: If someone treats their slave softly from childhood, the slave will eventually come to believe he is a son.
29.22: An angry man stirs up quarrels, and a violent man falls into many sins.
29.23: A man's pride leads him to humiliation, but the humble in spirit obtains glory.
29.24: He who shares with a thief hates his soul; he hears the curse and says nothing.
29.25: The fear of men carries with it a snare, but he who trusts in the Lord is safe.
29.26: Many people seek the favor of the prince, but justice comes to each one from the Lord.
29.27: The wicked man is an abomination to the righteous, and he whose way is upright is an abomination to the wicked.

30.1: The words of Agur, son of Jakeh, the saying. This man said: I have grown weary to know God, to know God, and I am at the end of my strength.
30.2: Because I am more stupid than anyone and I don't have the intelligence of a man.
30.3: I have not learned wisdom and I do not know the science of the Holy One.
30.4: Who ascends to heaven and who descends? Who gathers the wind in his hands? Who binds the waters in his garment? Who establishes all the ends of the earth? What is his name, and what is the name of his son? Surely you know?
